elements.sent-2411-001: We investigate a monostatic \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS system with multiple antennas for object tracking.}}
elements.sent-2411-002: The native resolution of \ac{OFDM sensing, and radar sensing in general, is limited by the observation time and bandwidth.}
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we improve the resolution through interpolation methods and tracking algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-004: We verify the resolution enhancement by comparing the \ac{RMSE of the estimated range, velocity and angle and by comparing the mean Euclidean distance between the estimated and true position.}
elements.sent-2411-005: We demonstrate how both a Kalman filter for tracking, and interpolation methods using zero-padding and the \ac{CZT improve the estimation error.}
elements.sent-2411-006: We discuss the computational complexity of the different methods.
elements.sent-2411-007: We propose the KalmanCZT approach that combines tracking via Kalman filtering and interpolation via the \ac{CZT , resulting in a solution with flexible resolution that significantly improves the range \ac{RMSE .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Different features of the environment can be sensed as the communication signals are reflected by passive objects resulting in multiple reflections of the same signal being received.
elements.sent-2411-009: These reflections can be identified and attributed to different objects in the environment, transforming devices for wireless communication into potential sensors.
elements.sent-2411-010: Different applications have been proposed including weather monitoring, network optimization, traffic assistance or drone detection.
elements.sent-2411-011: Additionally, an improvement in energy and spectral efficiency is expected compared to deploying separate radar and communication systems.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, precise radar estimates rely on sufficient observation time and a large signal bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-013: Bandwidth remains a finite resource as different systems operate simultaneously and interference has to be prevented.
elements.sent-2411-014: In 6G, we expect a limited bandwidth that is primarily chosen for communication, while a sensing functionality would benefit from a larger bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-016: In particular, the limited range resolution contributes severely to the estimation error when localizing passive objects.
elements.sent-2411-017: If we want to capture a trajectory in time, limited resolution makes a single estimate inaccurate.
elements.sent-2411-018: Methods such as tracking and interpolation offer more accurate estimates of the spatial environment.
elements.sent-2411-019: A well-designed interpolation approach improves the ability of the system to distinguish between closely spaced objects and increases the estimation precision, effectively increasing the detail of the sensed environment.
elements.sent-2411-020: Interpolation approaches for sensing include \ac{ZP , the \ac{CZT ~\cite{Xu2023 , or superresolution methods~\cite{Liu2020a .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-021: Tracking additionally helps to maintain consistent observations of moving targets, allowing for the prediction of their future positions, which is vital for navigation, collision avoidance, and beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-022: If positions follow a trajectory, we can use prediction to refine the estimates, as the positions along a trajectory are typically correlated.
elements.sent-2411-023: Numerous studies applying Kalman filters to object tracking are part of the radar literature, e.g. in FMCW radar~\cite{Lipka2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Especially, angle of arrival tracking has been of interest, since it enables precise beam tracking~\cite{Burghal2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-025: True position tracking can potentially further enhance energy-aware beam tracking.
elements.sent-2411-026: Additionally, object tracking is a basic task for \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS systems and therefore of interest~\cite{Sanson2021 in order to track, e.g., obstacles in traffic or drones.}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: In order to enable real-time tracking, the complexity of the resolution enhancement algorithm needs to be sufficiently low, yet it should yield a position estimate that is as accurate as possible.
elements.sent-2411-028: This paper specifically compares Kalman filtering structures and interpolation methods to improve tracking resolution in an \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS system.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: We discuss how the computational complexity can be limited and how the estimation results are affected by interpolation and tracking, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-030: We present an alternative event-based measurement approach and propose the KalmanCZT as a combination approach of interpolation and tracking.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this paper, we investigate a monostatic \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS setup, where the transmitter and the sensing receiver are co-located, i.e., part of the same base station, and have multiple antennas.}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Our goal is to track the \ac{AoA , range and radial velocity of an object based on its reflection to track its location in a 2D plane.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The transmit signal is simultaneously used to communicate with a \ac{UE equipped with a single antenna in a separate area of interest.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We simulate a split transmit beam to enable communication, yet focus on the object tracking in this work.
elements.sent-2411-035: We consider an \ac{OFDM signal with $N$ orthogonal sub-carriers with sub-carrier spacing $\Delta f = B/N = 1/(T_{\text{s N) = 1/T$, bandwidth $B$, sample duration $T_{\text{s }$, and \ac{OFDM symbol duration $T$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-036: The $NM$ transmit symbols of a frame are selected from the QPSK modulation alphabet and can be arranged as a matrix $\mat{X}$ of dimension $N\times M$.
elements.sent-2411-037: The notation $\mat{X [n,m]$ denotes the element of $\mat{X}$ in the $n$th row and $m$th column, corresponding to the symbol of the $n$th sub-carrier of the $m$th OFDM symbol in a frame.}
elements.sent-2411-038: A \ac{CP of duration $T_{\text{cp }$ is added to each symbol before transmitting the equivalent baseband signal~\cite{Braun2010 \begin{equation}s(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{m = 0}^{M-1} \sum_{n = 0}^{N-1} \mat{X}[n,m] \mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{j} 2\pi n \Delta f (t - m T_0)} g(t - mT_0),\end{equation} where $T_0 = T_{\text{cp +T$ and $g(t)=1$ for $t \in [-T_{\text{cp ,T]$ and $0$ elsewhere is a rectangular pulse shaping filter.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-001: \begin{equation}s(t) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N}} \sum_{m = 0}^{M-1} \sum_{n = 0}^{N-1} \mat{X}[n,m] e^{j 2\pi n \Delta f (t - m T_0)} g(t - mT_0),\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-039: We transmit the \ac{OFDM signal at carrier frequency $f_{\text{c }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The transmitter is equipped with $K$ antennas in a \ac{ULA with antenna spacing $c_0/2f_{\text{c }$, where $c_0$ represents the speed of light.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Beam squint is neglected in this configuration.
elements.sent-2411-042: After beamforming with the beamforming function $w_a(\varphi)$, with $\varphi$ being the \ac{AoA of the tracked object and antenna index $a\in[0,K\nobreak-1]$, the signal transmitted towards angle $\varphi$ can be expressed as \begin{equation}\vect{s}_{\varphi}(t) = \sum_{a=0}^{K-1} w_a(\varphi) s(t) e^{j\pi a\sin\varphi}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-043: A part of the transmit signal reaches the communication receiver while another part is reflected by the object of interest and reaches the sensing receiver co-located with the transmitter.
elements.sent-2411-044: The sensing channel is assumed to have one reflection caused by the tracked object with delay $\tau_{\mathrm{o }$,\footnote{We do not distinguish between random variables and scalars notation-wise to retain a clear and simple notation associated Doppler shift $f_{\mathrm{D ,{\mathrm{o }$ and factor $a_{\mathrm{o = 1$ representing the radar cross section, path loss and directivity gains.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The received signal is given by \begin{equation}r(t) = a_{\mathrm{o}} s(t - \tau_{\mathrm{o}}) \mathrm{e}^{ \mathrm{j} 2 \pi f_{\mathrm{D},{\mathrm{o}}} t }.\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}r(t) = a_{o} s(t - \tau_{o}) e^{ j 2 \pi f_{D,{o}} t }.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-046: Finally the signal $r(t)$ is normalized to the power $\sigma^2_{s}$ and then received by the different antennas, resulting in \begin{equation}\vect{r}_{\varphi,a}(t) = r(t) e^{ j \pi a\sin\varphi}+ q(t),\end{equation} with independent noise $q(t) \sim \mathcal{CN (0,\sigma^2_{\text{ns )$ at each antenna.}}}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics{external/paper-figure0} \caption{Scenario: An example trajectory of a passive object is shown with the corresponding position estimate using the native \ac{RDM}. The resolution of the \ac{RDM} is not able to accurately capture the object trajectory.} \label{fig:setup}
elements.sent-2411-047: At the sensing receiver, we first estimate the angle using the Bartlett method due to its computational complexity advantage in favor of more sophisticated methods~\cite{obeidat2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: Then, we perform receive beamforming based on the estimated angle $\varphi_{\text{est }$.}
elements.sent-2411-049: The received signal that is considered for estimating the radial velocity and the range is \begin{equation}\vect{r}_{\varphi_{est}}(t) = \sum_{a=0}^{K-1} w_a(\varphi_{est}) \vect{r}_{\varphi,a} e^{j\pi a\sin\varphi}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-050: We can define the instantaneous \ac{SNR for \ac{AoA estimation to $\text{SNR _{\varphi = \sigma_{\text s ^2 / \sigma_{\text{ns ^2$, with $\sigma_{\text{s ^2$ representing the end-to-end received signal power after effects such as path loss or the radar cross section.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-051: For estimating range and velocity, we follow the \ac{OFDM sensing processing from~\cite{Braun2014 , i.e., we remove the cyclic prefix and transform the received signal to the time-frequency domain.}}
elements.sent-2411-052: After matched filtering, we obtain the received symbol matrix $\mat{R}$ and with the corresponding transmitted matrix $\mat{X}$, we can compute the element-wise ratio matrix $\mat{Y =\mat{R}\oslash \mat{X}$, with $\oslash$ denoting the Hadamard division.}
elements.sent-2411-053: The \ac{RDM is calculated as \begin{equation}\mathrm{RDM} = \underset{N \downarrow}{\mathrm{IFFT}} \left\{ \underset{M \rightarrow}{\mathrm{FFT}} \left\{\mat{Y} \right\} \right\}, \label{eq:ch2:RDM}\end{equation} with $M \rightarrow$ denoting that the given operation is performed on each row of the input matrix and $N \downarrow$ denoting that the operation is performed on each column of the matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-054: We estimate the range and velocity as the values $\hat{r}$ and $\hat{v}$ corresponding to the element of the RDM with maximum power, since we assume that there is always one object present.
elements.sent-2411-055: In Fig.~\ref{fig:setup , an example trajectory and the resulting estimated positions are shown.}
elements.sent-2411-056: An \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS system performing sensing on the received frame, as described in~\cite{Braun2014 , suffers from a limited resolution, as the native 2D grid of the \ac{OFDM frame allows distinguishing between bins of spacing \begin{equation}r_{res} = \frac{c_0}{2 B}, v_{res} = \frac{c_0}{2 f_{c} MT_0}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: The computational complexity of the \ac{RDM can be inferred from the \ac{FFT complexity as $5NM\cdot\nobreak\log_2(NM)$ \acp{FLOP ~\cite[Table 2]{Chimmalgi2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The signal resolution can be improved through interpolation methods, including \ac{ZP of the frame or applying \acp{CZT , or by using time correlation in the positions and applying tracking algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: In this section, we will introduce these methods in detail.
elements.sent-2411-060: Both the required memory for the transform result as well as the computational effort for calculating the interpolated \ac{RDM increase with the \ac{ZP amount.}}
elements.sent-2411-061: As we are mostly interested in improving the range resolution, we zero-pad the frame by adding rows to the input matrix resulting in a frame of dimensions $N_{\text{pad \times M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: After \ac{ZP , we need to calculate $N_{\text{pad }$ \ac{IFFT values from $N$ nonzero entries per original column, while the complexity of the \ac{FFT per row doesn't change.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Therefore, the total complexity can be approximated as $5MN_{\text{pad }\log_2(N_{\text{pad ) + 5NM\log_2(M)$ \acp{FLOP .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: In our application, we are interested in the zoom capabilities of the transform, i.e., we use it to analyze a smaller section of the \ac{RDM in more detail.}
elements.sent-2411-065: We first need a good estimate of the area where the tracked object is located.
elements.sent-2411-066: We can get this information by either using the last estimate or a prediction based on past dynamics as the center of an observation window, or by detecting with a lower resolution before refining our estimate.
elements.sent-2411-067: The \ac{CZT as well as its inverse can be calculated with a complexity of $75(2N\log_2(2N))$ \acp{FLOP , assuming the number of output and input values is both $N$.}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Note that this is five times higher than the complexity reported for the \ac{CZT in~\cite[Table 2]{Chimmalgi2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-069: In~\cite{Chimmalgi2019 , the \ac{CZT complexity is based only on the complexity of the underlying FFTs.}}
elements.sent-2411-070: However, we observe that the costs of other operations such as exponentiation are also significant.
elements.sent-2411-072: In our application, we leverage the \ac{RDM estimate in the first time step and then for the subsequent steps, we zoom around the estimate of the previous time step.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The resulting transform to generate a zoomed in section is calculated by: \begin{equation}A = i_{est}/N_{FFT}-d/2, \quad W = e^{\left( j \frac{2\pi d}{N_{CZT}} \right)}\label{eq:cztsettings} RDM_{fine} = \underset{[A_{r},W_{r},N] \downarrow }{CZT} \left\{ \underset{[A_{v},W_{v},M] \rightarrow}{CZT} \left\{\mat{Y} \right\}^* \right\}^*,\end{equation} while $d$ invokes the index span that the zoomed transform is considering and $i_{\text{est }$ being the index in the middle of the zoomed frame.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}A = i_{est}/N_{FFT}-d/2, \quad W = e^{\left( j \frac{2\pi d}{N_{CZT}} \right)}\label{eq:cztsettings} RDM_{fine} = \underset{[A_{r},W_{r},N] \downarrow }{CZT} \left\{ \underset{[A_{v},W_{v},M] \rightarrow}{CZT} \left\{\mat{Y} \right\}^* \right\}^*,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-074: From the elements of $\mathrm{RDM _{\text{fine }$ with maximum power, the corresponding range and velocity estimates can be obtained.}}
elements.sent-2411-075: These are more precise than estimation using the native \ac{RDM .}
elements.sent-2411-076: In our scenario, we use the \ac{CZT to increase the range resolution and use the \ac{RDM to obtain the velocity estimate.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: The Kalman filter can be divided into two distinct phases: the prediction phase and the update phase.
elements.sent-2411-078: In the prediction phase, the filter uses the current estimate $\vect{x _{k-1}$ and the system dynamics $\mat{F}$ to predict the range, velocity and angle at the next time step.}
elements.sent-2411-079: With our model, the prediction equation of the Kalman filter in each time step $k$ is given by \begin{equation}\vect{x}_{k|k-1} =  r_{k|k-1} v_{k|k-1} \varphi_{k|k-1}  =\underbrace{ 1 -T_0 0 0 1 0 0 0 1 }_{\mat{F}} \cdot \vect{x}_{k-1},\label{eq:kalman_pred}\end{equation} with $\vect{x _{k-1}$ denoting the output estimate of the Kalman filter in time step $k-1$ and $r_{k|k-1}$, $v_{k|k-1}$, $\varphi_{k|k-1}$ denoting the predicted range, radial velocity and \ac{AoA , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: As the radial velocity is defined to be positive for movements towards the receiver, we predict the new range as $r_{k|k-1 =r_{k-1 -T_0v_{k-1}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-081: During the update phase, the filter incorporates new measurements to correct the predicted state, weighting the impact of the measurement with Kalman gain $\mat{K}$.
elements.sent-2411-082: By combining these two phases, the Kalman filter continuously refines its estimates, leading to an increased accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-083: The behavior of the system is influenced by the observation noise $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{meas ,k}$, modeling the noise caused by the resolution limit and the prediction noise $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{pred ,k}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: The computational complexity of a Kalman filter is $\mathcal{O _{\text{KF (s^3)$ with $s$ denoting the size of the state space (which is $3$ in our case).}}}
elements.sent-2411-085: In all other cases, we rely on the linear prediction assuming a constant velocity of the target.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics{external/paper-figure1} \caption{Flowgraph of the novel, proposed joint tracking and filtering algorithm KalmanCZT} \label{fig:kalmanCZT-flowgraph}
elements.sent-2411-086: We propose the KalmanCZT approach that combines tracking and filtering with a Kalman filter and the interpolation capabilities of the \ac{CZT .}
elements.sent-2411-087: We specifically address improving the range resolution of object tracking.
elements.sent-2411-088: A high-level block diagram of the KalmanCZT is shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:kalmanCZT-flowgraph .}
elements.sent-2411-089: As the Kalman filter estimates the covariance matrix $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est ,k|k-1}$ of the prediction, we use this estimate to steer the resolution of the \ac{CZT measurement, as well as using the predicted value $\vect{x _{k|k-1}$ as the center of the observation window of the \ac{CZT .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-090: In Algorithm~\ref{alg:kalmanCZT , the KalmanCZT estimator is detailed.}
elements.sent-2411-091: We first perform the prediction given by ~\eqref{eq:kalman_pred , then feed the output values as the center of the observation window to the \ac{CZT .}}
elements.sent-2411-092: The covariance matrix $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est ,k|k-1}$ indicates the accuracy of the state estimates; therefore we use it to define the observation window of the \ac{CZT .}}}
elements.sent-2411-093: As we want to ensure that the actual target range is in the observation window, we choose six times the standard deviation of the range as observation range $r_{\text{CZT }$, using the three-sigmas rule.}
elements.sent-2411-094: We add a minimum range of $1\,$cm.
elements.sent-2411-095: We calculate the \ac{CZT parameters $W_{\text{r ,k}$ according to this choice and $A_{\text{r ,k}$ according to the predicted range-Doppler estimate $\vect{x _{k|k-1}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-096: After applying the \ac{CZT , we extract the range measurement $r_{\text{est }$ and velocity measurement $v_{\text{est }$ corresponding to the delay-Doppler observation with maximum power and combine them into an estimation vector $\vect{z _k =\begin{pmatrix r_{\text{est v_{\text{est \varphi_{\text{est \end{pmatrix ^{\top}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-097: As we assume the measurement noise to be mostly attributed to the resolution $d_{\text{CZT ,k = r_{\text{CZT ,k / N_{\text{CZT ,1}$, we model it as uniform noise, resulting in the updated range variance \begin{equation}\mat{\Sigma}_{meas}[0,0] = \int_{-d_{CZT,k}/2}^{{d_{CZT,k}/2}} \frac{x^2}{d_{CZT,k}} \dif x = \frac{d^2_{CZT,k}}{12},\end{equation} while the other components of $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{meas }$ are not updated.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Finally, the Kalman gain $\mat{K}$ is updated based on the covariance matrices and the regular update step of the Kalman filter is performed.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{KalmanCZT algorithm}\label{alg:kalmanCZT} \begin{algorithmic} \State Initialize state estimate $\vect{x}_{\text{track},0}$, covariance $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},0|0}$ and prediction noise $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{pred}}$ \For{each time step $k$} \State Prediction step: \State $\vect{x}_{k|k-1} = \mat{F} \vect{x}_{k-1}$ \State $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1} = \mat{F} \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k-1|k-1} \mat{F}^T + \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{pred}}$ \State CZT measurement: \State $r_{\text{CZT},k} = \max \left(6 \sqrt{\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1}[0,0]},1\,\text{cm} \right)$ \State $d_{\text{CZT},k} = r_{\text{CZT},k} / N_{\text{CZT},1}$ \State $W_{\text{r},k} = \mathrm{e}^{(\mathrm{j}2\pi r_{\text{CZT},k} / (N_{\text{CZT},1} N r_{\text{res}}))}$ \State $A_{\text{r},k} = \mathrm{e}^{ \mathrm{j}2\pi \max\left\{0,\frac{\vect{x}_{k|k-1}[0]}{N r_{\text{res}}}-\frac{r_{\text{CZT},k}}{2 N r_{\text{res}}}\right\}}$ \State $\mat{Z}_k = \underset{[A_{\text{r},k},W_{\text{r},k},N_{\text{CZT},1}] \downarrow }{\mathrm{CZT}} \left\{ \underset{[A_{\text{v}},W_{\text{v}},N_{\text{CZT},2}] \rightarrow}{\mathrm{CZT}} \left\{\mat{R} \oslash \mat{X} \right\}^* \right\}^*$ \State $r_{\text{est}}, v_{\text{est}}=$ Peak power detection $\left(\mat{Z}_k\right)$ \State $\vect{z}_k = \left( r_{\text{est}}, v_{\text{est}}, \varphi_{\text{est}} \right)^{\top}$ \State $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{meas},k}[0,0] = \frac{d_{\text{CZT},k}^2}{12}$ \State Update step: \State $\mat{K}_k = \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1} ( \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1} + \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{meas},k})^{-1}$ \State $\vect{x}_k = \vect{x}_{k|k-1} + \mat{K}_k (\vect{z}_k - \vect{x}_{k|k-1})$ \State $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k} = (\mat{I} - \mat{K}_k ) \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-099: We use the \ac{CRB for benchmarking all sensing estimates.}
elements.sent-2411-100: The \ac{CRB is a fundamental lower bound of the estimation error, but does not consider time dependence of the parameters to be estimated.}
elements.sent-2411-101: In a tracking scenario, we may get an estimation accuracy below the \ac{CRB .}
elements.sent-2411-102: For the \ac{AoA , the \ac{CRB can be formulated as \begin{equation}\label{eq:CRB} Var({\varphi_{est}}-\varphi) = \frac{6\sigma_{ns}^2 \left(\sigma_{ns}^2 + K \sigma_{s^2} \right)}{\pi^2 \cos(\varphi)^2 N_{win} \sigma_{s}^4 K^2 (K^2-1)}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-103: We average over the full angle range using $\frac{1 {2\pi \int_{-\pi ^{\pi \cos(x)^2 \dif x = \frac 12$ to formulate an average \ac{CRB \begin{equation}\label{eq:CRBa} \overline{Var}(\varphi_{est}-\varphi) = \frac{12\sigma_{ns}^2 \left(\sigma_{ns}^2 + K \sigma_{s}^2 \right)}{\pi^2 N_{win} \sigma_{s}^4 K^2 (K^2-1)}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-104: For the range and velocity estimation bounds of the native \ac{RDM , we bound the error to the quantization noise introduced by the resolution limit instead of the \ac{CRB ~\cite{Braun2011 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-105: With the quantization introduced by the bins, the lower bounds are \begin{equation}Var(\hat{r}-r) \geq \frac{c_0^2}{12(2N_{FFT}\Delta f)^2}\label{eq:bound-range}\quad and Var(\hat{v}_{rad}-v_{rad}) \geq \frac{c_0^2}{12(2 f_c M_{FFT} T_0)^2}. \label{eq:crb-velocity}\end{equation}.
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}Var(\hat{r}-r) \geq \frac{c_0^2}{12(2N_{FFT}\Delta f)^2}\label{eq:bound-range}\quad and Var(\hat{v}_{rad}-v_{rad}) \geq \frac{c_0^2}{12(2 f_c M_{FFT} T_0)^2}. \label{eq:crb-velocity}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-106: We consider an \ac{OFDM system with carrier frequency $f_\text{c =5\,$GHz, a bandwidth of $B=25\,$MHz, and $N=2048$ subcarriers with a cyclic prefix length of $N_{\text{cp =30$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: We group $M=259$ OFDM symbols in one frame for sensing.
elements.sent-2411-108: Therefore, the bounds \eqref{eq:CRBa -\eqref{eq:crb-velocity in the chosen scenario can be calculated as \begin{equation}\sqrt{\overline{Var}({\varphi_{est}}-\varphi)} = 6.8 \cdot 10^{-4}\, (rad) \sqrt{{Var}(\hat{r}-r)} = 1.73\, (m) \sqrt{{Var}(\hat{v}_{rad}-{v}_{rad})} = 0.409\, (m/s).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-109: With the native \ac{RDM resolution, the range is the most limiting factor to reconstruct the trajectory accurately, as already indicated in Fig.~\ref{fig:setup .}}
elements.sent-2411-110: We choose $N_{\text{CZT =2048$ and use the complexity estimates in Tab.~\ref{tab:methods-comp-compl to calculate the required \ac{FFT length $N_{\text{pad =16N$ after \ac{ZP , such that \ac{ZP and the \ac{CZT have a similar complexity.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-111: To get the same range resolution in the \ac{CZT as in the \ac{ZP , we configure the \ac{CZT in~\eqref{eq:cztsettings at time step $k$ as \begin{equation}A_{v} =0, \quad W_{v}=e^{j2\pi M}, \quad M=259 \quad and A_{r} =e^{j2\pi \left(\frac{r_{k-1}}{Nr_{res}} - 2^{-5}\right) }, \quad W_{r}=e^{j\pi 2^{-14}}, \quad N_{CZT}=2^{11}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation}A_{v} =0, \quad W_{v}=e^{j2\pi M}, \quad M=259 \quad and A_{r} =e^{j2\pi \left(\frac{r_{k-1}}{Nr_{res}} - 2^{-5}\right) }, \quad W_{r}=e^{j\pi 2^{-14}}, \quad N_{CZT}=2^{11}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-112: The parameter ${r _{k-1}$ denotes the range estimated in the previous time step.}
elements.sent-2411-113: For the Kalman filter, the input values for the prediction and measurement covariance matrices significantly influence the behavior.
elements.sent-2411-114: Using experiments, we set them to \begin{equation}\mat{\Sigma}_{meas} = diag 4.4 0.01 0.01 ^{\top} ,  and \notag \mat{\Sigma}_{pred} = diag 1.3\cdot 10^{-5} 0.8 0.4 ^{\top} ,\end{equation} with $\text{diag (\cdot)$ denoting the diagonal matrix operator.}
elements.sent-2411-115: We initialize $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est =\mat{0}$ if the initial position is known and $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est =\text{diag}\begin{pmatrix r_{\text{res v_{\text{res \frac 12 \end{pmatrix ^{\top}$ if a measurement of the initial position is available.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-116: For the KalmanCZT, we choose the same initial $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{meas }$, $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est }$, $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{pred }$ and prediction matrix $\mat{F}$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: We show a complexity estimate for each method in Tab.~\ref{tab:methods-comp-compl .}
elements.sent-2411-118: The \ac{RDM has the lowest complexity, and we chose the parameters of \ac{ZP and the \ac{CZT to have similar complexity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-119: On the other hand, the memory requirement for \ac{ZP is $16$ times the memory required for the other approaches.}

elements.sent-2411-147: In this paper, we provide an analysis on resolution boosting methods with a focus on range estimation in \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-148: We compare tracking methods and interpolation methods and propose the KalmanCZT approach, that combines estimation based on the \ac{CZT with Kalman filtering.}
elements.sent-2411-149: The range estimation error can be reduced from $\approx\begin{equation}1.7\,$m to $\approx\end{equation}3\,$mm and the importance of tracking initialization is pointed out by comparing tracking with a known initial position and a known initial estimate.
elements.sent-2411-150: We provide a detailed complexity analysis, noting that the additional complexity for tracking algorithms is very low compared to the computation of the \ac{RDM .}
elements.sent-2411-151: Application of the \ac{CZT instead of the \ac{FFT yields improved resolution, for a controlled increase in computational complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-152: The KalmanCZT approach provides a flexible resolution controlled by the dynamics of the tracked object, enabling efficient use of the computational resources.. \acresetall \acused{ESPRIT \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
elements.sent-2411-153: We investigate a monostatic \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS system for object tracking.}}
elements.sent-2411-154: Our setup consists of a transmitter and receiver equipped with an antenna array for fully digital beamforming.
elements.sent-2411-155: The native resolution of all radar-like sensing, including \ac{OFDM radar sensing, is limited by the observation time and bandwidth.}
elements.sent-2411-156: In this work, we improve the parameter estimates through interpolation methods and tracking algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-157: We verify our method by comparing the \ac{RMSE of the estimated range, velocity and angle and by comparing the mean Euclidean distance between the estimated and true position.}
elements.sent-2411-158: Different features of the environment can be sensed as transmit signals are reflected by passive objects resulting in multiple reflections of the same signal being received.
elements.sent-2411-159: Additionally, it has been studied for application in radar for over a decade~\cite{Braun2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-160: In 6G, we expect a limited bandwidth that is primarily chosen for communication, limiting the sensing resolution.
elements.sent-2411-161: In this paper, we investigate a monostatic \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS setup, where the transmitter and the sensing receiver are co-located, i.e., part of the same base station, and are equipped with \acp{ULA to perform digital beamforming.}}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Our goal is to track the \ac{AoA , range and radial velocity of a passive object based on its reflection to track its location in a 2D plane.}
elements.sent-2411-163: This approach enables us to use samples beyond the pilots for sensing which increases the resolution for sensing.
elements.sent-2411-164: After beamforming with the beamforming function $\tilde{w _a=\mathrm{e ^{-\j\pi a\sin\varphi /2+\mathrm{e ^{-\j\pi a\sin\theta /2$, with $\varphi$ being the \ac{AoD of the tracked object, $\theta$ being the \ac{AoD of the communication receiver, and antenna index $a\in[0,K\nobreak-1]$, the signal transmitted towards angle $\varphi$ can be expressed as \begin{equation}\vect{s}_{\varphi}(t) = \sum_{a=0}^{K-1} \tilde{w}_a s(t) e^{\j\pi a\sin\varphi}.\end{equation}.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-165: A part of the transmit signal is received by the \ac{UE while another part is reflected by the object of interest and reaches the sensing receiver co-located with the transmitter.}
elements.sent-2411-166: The sensing channel is assumed to have one reflection caused by the tracked object with delay $\tau_{\mathrm{o }$,\footnote{We do not distinguish between random variables and scalars notation-wise to maintain readability associated Doppler shift $f_{\mathrm{D ,{\mathrm{o }$ and factor $a_{\mathrm{o = 1$ representing the radar cross section, path loss and directivity gains.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-167: The received signal is given by \begin{equation}r(t) = a_{\mathrm{o}} s(t - \tau_{\mathrm{o}}) \mathrm{e}^{ \j 2 \pi f_{\mathrm{D},{\mathrm{o}}} t }.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-168: Finally the signal $r(t)$ is normalized to the power $\sigma^2_{s}$ and then received by the different antennas, resulting in \begin{equation}\vect{r}_{\varphi,a}(t) = r(t) e^{\j\pi a\sin\varphi}+ q(t),\end{equation} with independent noise $q(t) \sim \mathcal{CN (0,\sigma^2_{\text{ns )$ at each antenna.}}}
elements.sent-2411-169: At the sensing receiver, we first estimate the angle $\varphi_{\text{est }$ using the Bartlett method due to its computational complexity advantage~\cite{obeidat2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-170: Then, we perform receive beamforming based on $\varphi_{\text{est }$.}
elements.sent-2411-171: The received signal that is considered for estimating the radial velocity and the range, with $w_a=\mathrm{e ^{-\j\pi a\sin\varphi_{\text{est }$, is \begin{equation}\vect{r}_{\varphi_{est}}(t) = \sum_{a=0}^{K-1} w_a(\varphi_{est}) \vect{r}_{\varphi,a} e^{\j\pi a\sin\varphi}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-172: We can define the instantaneous \ac{SNR for \ac{AoA estimation to $\text{SNR _{\varphi = \sigma_{\text s ^2 / \sigma_{\text{ns ^2$, with $\sigma_{\text{s ^2$ representing the end-to-end received signal power.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-173: The \acl{RDM (RDM) is calculated as \begin{equation}\mathrm{RDM} = \underset{N \downarrow}{\mathrm{IFFT}} \left\{ \underset{M \rightarrow}{\mathrm{FFT}} \left\{\mat{Y} \right\} \right\}, \label{eq:ch2:RDM}\end{equation} with $M \rightarrow$ denoting that the given operation is performed on each row of the input matrix and $N \downarrow$ denoting that the operation is performed on each column of the matrix.}
elements.sent-2411-174: This peak detection is used for all methods described in this work, a discussion of different detectors such as superresolution methods is omitted as of their computational complexity.
elements.sent-2411-175: In the initial estimate, we use the whole \ac{RDM as an observation window as we assume no knowledge of the initial position.}
elements.sent-2411-176: In the subsequent steps, we use the last estimate as the center of the observation window.
elements.sent-2411-177: The resulting transform to generate a zoomed in section is calculated by: \begin{equation}A = i_{est}/N_{FFT}-d/2, \quad W = e^{\left( \j \frac{2\pi d}{N_{CZT}} \right)}\label{eq:cztsettings} RDM_{fine} = \underset{[A_{r},W_{r},N] \downarrow }{CZT} \left\{ \underset{[A_{v},W_{v},M] \rightarrow}{CZT} \left\{\mat{Y} \right\}^* \right\}^*,\end{equation} while $d$ invokes the fraction of the \ac{FFT frame that the zoomed transform is considering and $i_{\text{est }$ being the index in the middle of the zoomed frame.}}
elements.sent-2411-178: In our scenario, we use the \ac{CZT to increase the range estimation accuracy.}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics{external/paper-figure1} \caption{Flowgraph of the proposed joint tracking and filtering algorithm KalmanCZT} \label{fig:kalmanCZT-flowgraph}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{KalmanCZT algorithm}\label{alg:kalmanCZT} \begin{algorithmic} \State Initialize state estimate $\vect{x}_{\text{track},0}$, covariance $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},0|0}$ and prediction noise $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{pred}}$ \For{each time step $k$} \State Prediction step: \State $\vect{x}_{k|k-1} = \mat{F} \vect{x}_{k-1}$ \State $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1} = \mat{F} \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k-1|k-1} \mat{F}^T + \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{pred}}$ \State CZT measurement: \State $r_{\text{CZT},k} = \max \left(6 \sqrt{\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1}[0,0]},1\,\text{cm} \right)$ \State $d_{\text{CZT},k} = r_{\text{CZT},k} / N_{\text{CZT},1}$ \State $W_{\text{r},k} = \mathrm{e}^{(\j2\pi r_{\text{CZT},k} / (N_{\text{CZT},1} N r_{\text{res}}))}$ \State $A_{\text{r},k} = \mathrm{e}^{ \j2\pi \max\left\{0,\frac{\vect{x}_{k|k-1}[0]}{N r_{\text{res}}}-\frac{r_{\text{CZT},k}}{2 N r_{\text{res}}}\right\}}$ \State $\mat{Z}_k = \underset{[A_{\text{r},k},W_{\text{r},k},N_{\text{CZT},1}] \downarrow }{\mathrm{CZT}} \left\{ \underset{[A_{\text{v}},W_{\text{v}},N_{\text{CZT},2}] \rightarrow}{\mathrm{CZT}} \left\{\mat{R} \oslash \mat{X} \right\}^* \right\}^*$ \State $r_{\text{est}}, v_{\text{est}}=$ Peak power detection $\left(\mat{Z}_k\right)$ \State $\vect{z}_k = \left( r_{\text{est}}, v_{\text{est}}, \varphi_{\text{est}} \right)^{\top}$ \State $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{meas},k}[0,0] = \frac{d_{\text{CZT},k}^2}{12}$ \State Update step: \State $\mat{K}_k = \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1} ( \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1} + \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{meas},k})^{-1}$ \State $\vect{x}_k = \vect{x}_{k|k-1} + \mat{K}_k (\vect{z}_k - \vect{x}_{k|k-1})$ \State $\mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k} = (\mat{I} - \mat{K}_k ) \mat{\Sigma}_{\text{est},k|k-1}$ \EndFor \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-179: For the \ac{AoA , the \ac{CRB can be formulated according to~\cite[Ch.. 8.4]{Trees2002 as \begin{equation}\label{eq:CRB} Var({\varphi_{est}}-\varphi) = \frac{6\sigma_{ns}^2 \left(\sigma_{ns}^2 + K \sigma_{s^2} \right)}{\pi^2 \cos(\varphi)^2 N_{win} \sigma_{s}^4 K^2 (K^2-1)}.\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Therefore, the bounds \eqref{eq:CRBa -\eqref{eq:crb-velocity in the chosen scenario of $\mathrm{SNR _{\varphi =0\,$dB can be calculated as \begin{equation}\sqrt{\overline{Var}({\varphi_{est}}-\varphi)} = 6.8 \cdot 10^{-4}\, (rad) \sqrt{{Var}(\hat{r}-r)} = 1.73\, (m) \sqrt{{Var}(\hat{v}_{rad}-{v}_{rad})} = 0.409\, (m/s).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-181: With the native \ac{RDM resolution, the range is the most limiting factor to reconstruct the trajectory accurately while the \ac{AoA can be estimated quite accurately, as already indicated in Fig.~\ref{fig:setup .}}}
elements.sent-2411-182: To get the same range resolution in the \ac{CZT as in the \ac{ZP , we configure the \ac{CZT in~\eqref{eq:cztsettings at time step $k$ as \begin{equation}A_{v} =0, \quad W_{v}=e^{\j2\pi M}, \quad M=259 \quad and A_{r} =e^{\j2\pi \left(\frac{r_{k-1}}{Nr_{res}} - 2^{-5}\right) }, \quad W_{r}=e^{\j\pi 2^{-14}}, \quad N_{CZT}=2^{11}.\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-023: \begin{equation}A_{v} =0, \quad W_{v}=e^{\j2\pi M}, \quad M=259 \quad and A_{r} =e^{\j2\pi \left(\frac{r_{k-1}}{Nr_{res}} - 2^{-5}\right) }, \quad W_{r}=e^{\j\pi 2^{-14}}, \quad N_{CZT}=2^{11}.\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-183: We initialize $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est =\mat{0}$ if the initial position is known and $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est =\text{diag}\begin{pmatrix r_{\text{res v_{\text{res \frac 12 \end{pmatrix ^{\top}$ if an estimate using the native \ac{RDM of the initial position is available.}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-185: In particular, the limited range resolution contributes severely to the estimation error when localizing objects that do not communicate.
elements.sent-2411-186: Interpolation approaches for sensing include \ac{ZP , the \ac{CZT ~\cite{Xu2023 , or super-resolution methods~\cite{Liu2020a .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-187: This paper specifically compares Kalman filtering structures and interpolation methods to improve tracking estimation in an \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS system.}}
elements.sent-2411-188: This approach enables us to use samples beyond the pilots for sensing which increases the accuracy for sensing.
elements.sent-2411-189: In this work, we assume a genie-aided beamformer to isolate the performance of the receiver.

elements.sent-2411-191: The complexity of computing the \ac{RDM can be inferred from the \ac{FFT complexity as $5NM\cdot\nobreak\log_2(NM)$ \acp{FLOP ~\cite[Table 2]{Chimmalgi2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-192: Signal estimation can be improved through interpolation methods, including \ac{ZP of the frame or applying \acp{CZT , or by using time correlation in the positions and applying tracking algorithms.}}
elements.sent-2411-193: As we are mostly interested in improving the range estimation, we zero-pad the frame by adding rows to the input matrix resulting in a frame of dimensions $N_{\text{pad \times M$.}}
elements.sent-2411-194: The resulting transform to generate a zoomed in section is calculated using: \begin{equation}RDM_{fine} = \underset{[A_{r},W_{r},N] \downarrow }{CZT} \left\{ \underset{[A_{v},W_{v},M] \rightarrow}{CZT} \left\{\mat{Y} \right\}^* \right\}^*, \label{eq:cztsettings}\end{equation} where the parameters are chosen as $W_{\text{r = \mathrm{e ^{\j2\pi r_{\text{CZT / ( N^2 r_{\text{res )}$ $A_{\text{r = \mathrm{e ^{ \j2\pi \left(\frac{\hat{r {N r_{\text{res -\frac{r_{\text{CZT {2 N r_{\text{res }\right)}$, $W_{\text{v =\mathrm{e ^{\j2\pi/M}$ and $A_{\text{v =-1$, with the last range estimate $\hat{r}$ as the center of the range observation window of size $r_{\text{CZT }$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-195: The range and velocity estimates corresponding to the element of $\mathrm{RDM _{\text{fine }$ with maximum power can then be obtained.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: With our model, the prediction equation of the Kalman filter at time step $k$ is given by \begin{equation}\vect{x}_{k|k-1} =  r_{k|k-1} v_{k|k-1} \varphi_{k|k-1}  =\underbrace{ 1 -T_0 0 0 1 0 0 0 1 }_{\mat{F}} \cdot \vect{x}_{k-1},\label{eq:kalman_pred}\end{equation} with $\vect{x _{k-1}$ denoting the output estimate of the Kalman filter in time step $k-1$ and $r_{k|k-1}$, $v_{k|k-1}$, $\varphi_{k|k-1}$ denoting the predicted range, radial velocity and \ac{AoA , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-197: The behavior of the system is influenced by the observation noise $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{meas ,k}$, the noise caused by the resolution limit and the prediction noise $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{pred ,k}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-198: We specifically address improving the range estimation of object tracking.
elements.sent-2411-199: As the Kalman filter estimates the covariance matrix $\mat{\Sigma _{\text{est ,k|k-1}$ of the prediction, we use this estimate to determine the observation window of the \ac{CZT , as well as using the predicted value $\vect{x _{k|k-1}$ as the center of the observation window of the \ac{CZT .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-200: All simulation scenarios assume a constant $\mathrm{SNR _{\varphi =0\,$dB, independent of range variation.}}
elements.sent-2411-201: Therefore, the bounds \eqref{eq:CRBa -\eqref{eq:crb-velocity can be calculated as \begin{equation}\sqrt{\overline{Var}({\varphi_{est}}-\varphi)} = 6.8 \cdot 10^{-4}\, (rad) \sqrt{{Var}(\hat{r}-r)} = 1.73\, (m) \sqrt{{Var}(\hat{v}_{rad}-{v}_{rad})} = 0.409\, (m/s).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-202: With the native \ac{RDM resolution, the range is the most limiting factor to reconstruct the trajectory accurately while the \ac{AoA can be estimated quite accurately, as illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig:setup .}}}

elements.sent-2411-212: In this paper, we provide an analysis on estimation accuracy enhancing methods with a focus on range estimation in \ac{OFDM -based \ac{JCAS systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-213: Application of the \ac{CZT instead of the \ac{FFT yields improved estimation, for a controlled increase in computational complexity.}}
elements.sent-2411-214: The KalmanCZT approach provides a flexible estimation controlled by the dynamics of the tracked object, enabling efficient use of the computational resources.
elements.sent-2411-215: For future investigations we are planning to extend the approach in order to apply it to real measurement data.. \acresetall \acused{ESPRIT \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}}
elements.sent-2411-216: The native resolution of range and velocity in all radar-like sensing, including \ac{OFDM radar sensing, is limited by the observation time and bandwidth.}
elements.sent-2411-217: In this work, we improve the parameter estimates (estimates of range) through interpolation methods and tracking algorithms.
elements.figure-2411-011: \includegraphics{external/paper-figure0.pdf} \caption{Scenario: An example trajectory of a passive object is shown with the corresponding position estimate using the native \ac{RDM}. The resolution of the \ac{RDM} is not able to accurately capture the object trajectory.} \label{fig:setup}
elements.sent-2411-218: With our model, the prediction equation of the Kalman filter at time step $k$ is given by \begin{equation}\vect{x}_{k|k-1} =  r_{k|k-1} v_{k|k-1} \varphi_{k|k-1}  =\underbrace{ 1 -MT_0 0 0 1 0 0 0 1 }_{\mat{F}} \cdot \vect{x}_{k-1},\label{eq:kalman_pred}\end{equation} with $\vect{x _{k-1}$ denoting the output estimate of the Kalman filter in time step $k-1$ and $r_{k|k-1}$, $v_{k|k-1}$, $\varphi_{k|k-1}$ denoting the predicted range, radial velocity and \ac{AoA , respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-219: As the radial velocity is defined to be positive for movements towards the receiver, we predict the new range as $r_{k|k-1 =r_{k-1 -MT_0v_{k-1}$.}}
